analogy: Think of "Indian Sociologists" through the chapter's own opening images: 82 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY CHAPTER 5 INDIAN SOCIOLOGISTS As you saw in the opening chapter of your first book, Introducing Sociology, the discipline is a relatively young one even in the European context, having been established only about a century ago. In India, interest in sociological... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Indian Sociologists is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 82 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY CHAPTER 5 INDIAN SOCIOLOGISTS As you saw in the opening chapter of your first book, Introducing Sociology, the discipline is a relatively young one even in the European context, having been established only about a century ago.

The text explains that in India, interest in sociological ways of thinking is a little more than a century old, but formal university teaching of sociology only began in 1919 at the University of Bombay.

The text explains that in the 1920s, two other universities — those at Calcutta and Lucknow — also began programmes of teaching and research in sociology and anthropology.

The text explains that today, every major university has a department of sociology, social anthropology or anthropology, and often more than one of these disciplines is represented.

The text explains that now-a-days sociology tends to be taken for granted in India, like most established things.

The text explains that in the early days, it was not clear at all what an Indian sociology would look like, and indeed, whether India really needed something like sociology.

The text explains that in the first quarter of the 20th century, those who became interested in the discipline had to decide for themselves what role it could play in India.

The text explains that in this chapter, you are going to be introduced to some of the founding figures of Indian sociology.

The text explains that these scholars have helped to shape the discipline and adapt it to our historical and social context.

The text explains that the specificity of the Indian context raised many questions.

The text explains that first of all, if western sociology emerged as an attempt to make sense of modernity, what would its role be in a country like India?

The text explains that india, too, was of course experiencing the changes brought about by moder nity but with an important difference — it was a colony.

The text explains that the first experience of modernity in India was closely intertwined with the experience of colonial subjugation.

The text explains that secondly, if social anthropology in the west arose out of the curiosity felt by European society about primitive cultures, what role could it have in India, which was an ancient and advanced civilisation, but which also had ‘primitive’ societies within it?

The text explains that finally, what useful role could sociology have in a sovereign, independent India, a nation about to begin its adventure with planned development and democracy?

The text explains that 8 3 INDIAN SOCIOLOGISTS The pioneers of Indian sociology not only had to find their own answers to questions like these, they also had to for mulate new questions for themselves.

The text explains that the British government wanted similar surveys done in all the princely states as well as the presidency areas directly under its control.

The text explains that for this reason, they felt that the state had a duty to protect the tribes and to help them sustain their way of life and culture, which were facing constant pressure to assimilate with mainstream Hindu culture.

The text explains that 1924: Appointed Lecturer in the Department of Economics and Sociology at Lucknow University 1938-41 Served as Director of Information under the first Congress-led government of the United Provinces of British India (present day Uttar Pradesh).

The text explains that internal and external sources of change are always present in every society.

The text explains that the most commonly cited internal source of change in western societies is the economy, but this source has not been as effective in India.

The text explains that but the resilience of tradition ensures that the pressure of conflict produces change in the tradition without breaking it.

The text explains that desai is one of the rare Indian sociologists who was directly involved in politics as a formal member of political parties.

The text explains that indian Sociology: Reflections and Introspections, Popular Prakashan, Bombay.

The text explains that pathways: Approaches to the Study of Society in India.

The text explains that pATEL, SUJATA. ‘Towards a Praxiological Understanding of Indian Society: The Sociology of A.R.

The text explains that desai’, in Uberoi, Sundar and Deshpande (eds) (in press).

The text explains that development Department, Government of West Bengal.

The text explains that 1987. ‘The Indian Village: Myth and Reality’ in the Dominant Caste and other Essays.

The text explains that uBEROI, PATRICIA, NANDINI SUNDAR AND SATISH DESHPANDE (eds) (2007).

The text explains that disciplinary Biographies: Essays in the History of Indian Sociology and Social Anthropology.

The text explains that uPADHYA, CAROL. ‘The Idea of Indian Society: G.S.

The text explains that ghurye and the Making of Indian Sociology’, in Uberoi, Sundar and Deshpande (eds) (2007).

Source: NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society, Chapter 5: Indian Sociologists

schemes.name: The British
schemes.point: The British government wanted similar surveys done in all the princely states as well as the presidency areas directly under its control. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 1)

schemes.name: These
schemes.point: These were the active combining of teaching and research within the same institution, and the merger of social anthropology and sociology into a composite discipline. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 2)

schemes.name: Programme
schemes.point: Programme was launched at the Bombay Department; the first Ph.D. in Sociology at an Indian university was awarded to G.R. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 3)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The M.A. course was revised and made a full-fledged 8-course programme in 1945. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 3)

schemes.name: Caste
schemes.point: (i) Caste is an institution based on segmental division. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 6)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 82 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY and CHAPTER 5 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Indian Sociologists; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Indian Sociologists through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 82 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY and CHAPTER 5 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Indian Sociologists, which statement is supported by the source text about 84 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY?
options: It was only through the experience of ‘doing’ sociology in an Indian context that the questions took shape — they were not available ‘readymade’.
